content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission


June 23, 1993


Vanessa Poster
804 El Redondo
Redondo Beach, California 90277

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-93-247

Dear Ms. Poster:

This is in response to your request for confirmation of
telephone advice provided to you concerning the propriety of using
campaign funds under the "personal use" provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ This letter is based on facts
provided in your letter dated June 14, 1993, and our telephone
conversation that same date.

QUESTION

May you use campaign funds or surplus campaign funds to make
a donation to the South Bay Free Clinic, a nonprofit charitable
organization in the City of Redondo Beach?

CONCLUSION

You may use campaign funds to make a donation to the South
Bay Free Clinic if all of the following apply: (1) the
organization is a bona fide charitable, educational, civic,
religious, or similar tax-exempt nonprofit organization; (2) no
substantial part of the proceeds of the donation will have a


_______________

¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
will be to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000 et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations. Sections 85800-
85807, which describe the permissible use of campaign funds, are
referred to as the "personal use" laws.


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95812 • (916) 322-5660

Our File No. A-93-247
Page 2


material financial effect on you, your campaign treasurer, any
individuals with authority to approve the expenditure of campaign
funds held by your committee, or any member of your immediate
family; and (3) the donation of campaign funds bears a reasonable
relation to a political, legislative, or governmental purpose.

     In addition, you may use surplus campaign funds to make a
donation to the South Bay Free Clinic irrespective of the purpose
of the donation, so long as the organization is a bona fide
charitable, educational, civic, religious, or similar tax-exempt
nonprofit organization, and no substantial part of the proceeds of
the donation will have a material financial effect on you, any
member of your immediate family, or your campaign treasurer.

FACTS

     You recently were an unsuccessful candidate in an election
for the Redondo Beach City Council.  You would like to make a
donation to the South Bay Free Clinic (SBFC) which is a prominent
nonprofit organization serving the community in the City of
Redondo Beach.  SBFC is one of the largest local charity
organizations in the city Redondo Beach.  You believe that it is
within your best interest politically to support financially such
an organization.

     You are also employed by the SBFC as Director of Development.
Your salary is set and does not change depending on donations
received.  You state you will not receive any personal financial
benefit from the donation, nor will anyone who was involved in
your campaign.

ANALYSIS

     The general rule of the personal use provisions of the Act is
that an expenditure of campaign funds² must be, at a minimum,
reasonably related to a political, legislative, or governmental
purpose.  (Section 89512.5.)  Where an expenditure
of campaign funds confers a substantial personal benefit on a
candidate, the expenditure must be directly related to a
political, legislative, or governmental purpose.  "Substantial
personal benefit" means an expenditure of campaign funds which
results in a direct personal benefit with a value of more than
$100 to the candidate.  (Section 89511(b)(3).)

     In other sections, the personal use law provides express
standards for specific types of expenditures.  For example,
expenditures for donations from campaign funds are governed by

_________________________
     2 "Campaign funds" which may be donated include cash, cash
equivalents, and other assets of the committee.  (Section
89511(b)(1).)

Our File No. A-93-247
Page 3


Section 89515.  In addition, where campaign funds become surplus
campaign funds, the personal use law limits the use of the funds
to those specified in Section 89519.  Campaign funds are
considered surplus campaign funds on the date on which a candidate
or elected officer leaves any elective office for which the
campaign funds were raised, or the end of the post-election
reporting period following the defeat of the candidate or elected
officer, whichever is later.  (Section 89519; See also, Regulation
18587.3)

     You ask regarding the propriety of using campaign funds to
make a donation to a nonprofit charitable organization in the City
of Redondo Beach.  Campaign funds may be used to make donations to
nonprofit organizations if all of the following apply:  (1) the
recipient organization is a bona fide charitable, educational,
civic, religious, or similar tax-exempt nonprofit organization;
(2) no substantial part of the proceeds will have a material
financial effect on the candidate, elected officer, campaign
treasurer, or any individual or individuals with authority to
approve the expenditure of campaign funds held by a committee, or
any member of his or her immediate family, and (3) the donation or
loan bears a reasonable relation to a political, legislative, or
governmental purpose.  (Section 89515.)

     According to your letter, the donation will benefit you
politically in the community where you ran for office.  Thus, it
would appear that this expenditure is reasonably related to a
political purpose which is a permissible expenditure of campaign
funds.

     You state in your letter that you were defeated in an
election for the Redondo Beach City Council.  Thus, your campaign
funds will be considered surplus campaign funds at the end of the
post-election period following the election.  (Section 89519 and
Regulation 18517.)

     Surplus campaign funds may be donated to nonprofit
organizations irrespective of the purpose of the donation, so long
as the recipient organization is a bona fide charitable,
educational, civic, religious, or similar tax-exempt nonprofit
organization, and no substantial part of the proceeds of the
donation will have a material financial effect on the candidate or
elected officer, any member of his or her immediate family, or his
or her campaign treasurer.  (Section 89519(c) and Milman Advice
Letter, No. I-92-240.)  Thus, so long as the funds are not used to
benefit you, any member of your immediate family, or your campaign
treasurer the donation to the nonprofit is a permissible use of
surplus campaign funds.


     3 Regulation 18587 was adopted by the Commission on
November 7, 1990, and is currently in the rulemaking process.
The regulation may be renumbered in the future.

Our File No. A-93-247
Page 4


According to your facts, SBFC is a nonprofit organization
serving the community in the City of Redondo Beach. You state in
your letter that you will not receive any personal financial
benefit from the donation, nor will anyone who was involved in
your campaign. Thus, assuming that the requirements of the
statute are complied with and you do not control the disposition
of the funds once the funds are provided to the nonprofit, the
donation of funds would be appropriate pursuant to Section 89519.4

If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Jeff Marschner
General Counsel

[signature]
By: Luisa Menchaca
Counsel, Legal Division




4 We would suggest that the express terms of Section 89519(c) be
incorporated into the letter submitted with your donation to SBFC
to avoid other uses that may have a material financial effect on
you, your immediate family, or your treasurer.
